summary: Gruber argues that the iPad Pro represents a fundamental inflection point where Apple's ARM-based tablets have caught up to and surpassed Intel x86 MacBooks in raw performance, battery life, and price. He contends the device is no longer a lightweight alternative to a laptop but a genuine peer, backed by benchmark comparisons showing the A9X rivaling a maxed-out MacBook Pro. While praising the hardware as seminal, he details significant software shortcomings — iOS's keyboard navigation is buggy and incomplete, making the Smart Keyboard experience frustrating for power users accustomed to Mac workflows. He concludes that the future of mass-market portable computing belongs to ARM and touch, not x86 and mouse pointers, even if iOS needs to mature considerably to fulfill that promise.
key_points: The iPad Pro matches or exceeds current MacBooks in CPU/GPU benchmarks while costing less and delivering better battery life, signaling that the x86 architecture is 'living on borrowed time'

iOS's keyboard navigation support is deeply inadequate — Safari's space-bar paging is broken, arrow key navigation is inconsistent across apps, and there's no trackpad to compensate for the lack of touch-friendly keyboard shortcuts
The Apple Pencil is technically remarkable with sub-pixel precision and 240Hz sampling, but will likely go largely unused by most buyers who aren't professional artists
iPad Pro is 'pro' in the MacBook Pro sense — not strictly for professionals, but a higher-end option suitable for anyone who wants the best iPad experience
The shift Gruber identifies is from iPads being lightweight alternatives for simple scenarios to MacBooks becoming heavyweight alternatives for complex scenarios
money_quotes: The entire x86 computer architecture is living on borrowed time. It's a dead platform walking. The future belongs to ARM, and Apple's A-series SoC's are leading the way.
You're going to have to forget your first impressions of the iPad to understand the iPad Pro.
There is no more trade-off. You don't have to choose between the performance of x86 and the battery life of ARM.
It brings me no joy to observe this, but the future of mass market portable computing involves neither a mouse pointer nor an x86 processor.

For me, the iPad Pro marks the turning point where iPads are no longer merely lightweight alternatives to MacBooks for use in simple scenarios, to where MacBooks will now start being seen as heavyweight alternatives to iPads for complex scenarios.
The user should never have to wait for the computer, especially if it's just for an animation.
Anyone who doesn't see this is blinded by their established impressions of the first few iPads.
What has been largely abstract — drawing using a device over here, looking at the results on a screen over there — can now be direct.
key_insight: The iPad Pro's ARM chip matching x86 MacBook performance at lower cost and better battery life marks the moment tablets stopped being lightweight laptop alternatives and started making laptops the heavyweight alternative.
